summary:
The Rattanakosin Kingdom (1782–present, though the dynastic name changes to modern 'Thailand') was established when General Chakri overthrew the [[event:3247|Thonburi Kingdom's]] troubled last ruler Taksin, crowned himself Rama I, and moved the capital from Thonburi across the Chao Phraya River to Bangkok — a decision that would transform a minor trading settlement into one of Southeast Asia's great cities. Rama I consciously modeled Bangkok as a successor to the lost Ayutthaya, naming the city Krung Thep ('City of Angels') and constructing the Grand Palace, the Emerald Buddha temple (Wat Phra Kaew), and city walls that replicated Ayutthaya's architectural tradition on an island formed by canals dug from the river.

The Rattanakosin period saw Siam navigate the challenges of European colonialism more successfully than most Southeast Asian states. Through astute diplomacy and strategic modernization under Rama IV (Mongkut, r. 1851–1868) and Rama V (Chulalongkorn, r. 1868–1910), Siam avoided direct colonization by Britain (which controlled Burma and Malaya) and France (which controlled Indochina), instead serving as a buffer state between the two empires and signing unequal treaties that preserved formal independence while ceding territory and commercial privileges. The foundation laid by the early Rattanakosin kings — administrative consolidation, Buddhist cultural revival, and trade network reconstruction — gave Siam the stability to undertake this difficult balancing act. Modern Thailand, the only Southeast Asian nation never formally colonized, traces its continuous political tradition to Rama I's establishment of Bangkok in 1782.
